description_plain: Your child can show the fraction 1/b on a number line by dividing the space from 0 to 1 into b equal parts.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction as a number on the number line; represent fractions on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child understands fractions as numbers on a number line and can show fractions on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction 1/b as the quantity formed by 1 part when a whole is partitioned into b equal parts (example: 1 part out of 4 equal parts is the same as ¼); understand a fraction a/b as the quantity formed by a parts of size 1/b. (example: ¾ is the same as 3 one-fourths (¼, ¼, ¼)
description_plain: Your child understands that 1/b means one part when a whole is split into b equal parts, and a/b means a parts of size 1/b.

subject: Math
description: Multiply one-digit whole numbers by multiples of 10 in the range 10–90 (e.g., 9 × 80, 5 × 60) using strategies based on place value and properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can multiply one-digit numbers by multiples of 10 (like 9 × 80) using place value and math rules.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract within 1000 using strategies and algorithms based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers up to 1000 using place value and math strategies.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding to round whole numbers to the nearest 10 or 100.
description_plain: Your child can round whole numbers to the nearest 10 or 100 using place value.

subject: Math
description: Partition shapes into parts with equal areas. Express the area of each part as a unit fraction of the whole. For example, partition a shape into 4 parts with equal area, and describe the area of each part as ¼ of the area of a shape.
description_plain: Your child can divide shapes into equal parts and say the area of each part as a fraction of the whole shape.

subject: Math
description: Understand that shapes in different categories (e.g., rhombuses, rectangles, and others) may share attributes (e.g., having four sides), and that the shared attributes can define a larger category (e.g., quadrilaterals). Recognize rhombuses, rectangles, and squares as examples of quadrilaterals, and draw examples of quadrilaterals that do not belong to any of these subcategories.
description_plain: Your child knows that shapes like rhombuses, rectangles, and squares share some features, like having four sides, and can name and draw shapes that fit into bigger groups like quadrilaterals.

subject: Math
description: Generate measurement data by measuring lengths using rulers marked with halves and fourths of an inch. Show the data by making a line plot, where the horizontal scale is marked off in appropriate units— whole numbers, halves, or quarters.
description_plain: Your child can measure lengths using rulers marked in halves and quarters of an inch and make line plots to show the data.

subject: Math
description: Draw a scaled picture graph and a scaled bar graph to represent a data set with several categories. Solve one- and two-step "how many more" and "how many less" problems using information presented in scaled bar graphs.
description_plain: Your child can draw picture and bar graphs with scales to show data and solve problems about how many more or less in the graphs.

subject: Math
description: Determine the value of a collection of money using dollar sign and decimal point appropriately. Understand that the digits to the right of the decimal represent parts of a whole dollar.
description_plain: Your child can find the total value of a group of money using the dollar sign and decimal point correctly, understanding that numbers after the decimal show parts of a dollar.

subject: Math
description: Solve real world and mathematical problems involving perimeters of polygons, including finding the perimeter given the side lengths, finding an unknown side length, and exhibiting rectangles with the same perimeter and different areas or with the same area and different perimeters.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about the perimeter of shapes, including finding missing side lengths and comparing rectangles with the same perimeter or area.

subject: Math
description: Recognize area as additive. Find areas of rectilinear figures by decomposing them into non-overlapping rectangles and adding the areas of the non-overlapping parts, applying this technique to solve real world problems.
description_plain: Your child knows that area adds up and can find the area of shapes made of rectangles by adding their parts.

subject: Math
description: Use tiling to show in a concrete case that the area of a rectangle with whole-number side lengths a and b + c is the sum of a × b and a × c. Use area models to represent the distributive property in mathematical reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can use tiles or drawings to show that the area of a rectangle with side lengths a and b + c equals the sum of areas a × b and a × c.

subject: Math
description: Multiply side lengths to find areas of rectangles with whole number side lengths in the context of solving real world and mathematical problems, and represent whole-number products as rectangular areas in mathematical reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of rectangles by multiplying side lengths and use this to solve real-world problems.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of a rectangle with whole-number side lengths by tiling it, and show that the area is the same as would be found by multiplying the side lengths.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of a rectangle by covering it with tiles and knows this is the same as multiplying its side lengths.

subject: Math
description: Relate area to the operations of multiplication and addition.
description_plain: Your child understands how area relates to multiplication and addition.

subject: Math
description: Measure areas by counting unit squares (square cm, square m, square in, square ft, and improvised units).
description_plain: Your child can measure area by counting square units like square centimeters or square inches.

subject: Math
description: A plane figure which can be covered without gaps or overlaps by n unit squares is said to have an area of n square units.
description_plain: Your child knows that if a shape can be covered exactly by n unit squares, its area is n square units.

subject: Math
description: A square with side length 1 unit, called "a unit square," is said to have "one square unit" of area, and can be used to measure area.
description_plain: Your child understands that a square with sides one unit long is called a unit square and has an area of one square unit.

subject: Math
description: Recognize area as an attribute of plane figures and understand concepts of area measurement.
description_plain: Your child recognizes area as a property of flat shapes and understands how to measure it.

subject: Math
description: Measure and estimate liquid volumes and masses of objects using standard units of grams (g), kilograms (kg), and liters (l). (Excludes compound units such as cm³ and finding the geometric volume of a container.) Add, subtract, multiply, or divide to solve one-step word problems involving masses or volumes that are given in the same units, e.g., by using drawings (such as a beaker with a measurement scale) to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can measure and estimate liquid volumes and weights using grams, kilograms, milliliters, and liters.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time to the nearest minute and measure time intervals in minutes, using an analog and digital clock. Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of time intervals in minutes, e.g., by representing the problem on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can tell and write time to the nearest minute using both analog and digital clocks and solve problems about time by adding or subtracting minutes.

subject: Math
description: Identify arithmetic patterns (including patterns in the addition table or multiplication table), and explain them using properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can find patterns in addition and multiplication tables and explain them using math rules.

subject: Math
description: Solve two-step word problems using the four operations. Represent these problems using equations with a letter standing for the unknown quantity. Assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies including rounding. (This standard is limited to problems posed with whole numbers and having whole number answers; students should know how to perform operations in the conventional order when there are no parentheses to specify a particular order [Order of Operations]).
description_plain: Your child can solve two-step word problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division, write equations with a letter for the unknown, and check answers by estimating.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate fluency (skill in carrying out procedures flexibly, appropriately, efficiently, and accurately) for all products of two one-digit numbers.
description_plain: Your child can quickly and accurately multiply any two one-digit numbers.

subject: Math
description: Fluently multiply and divide within 100, using strategies such as the relationship between multiplication and division (e.g., knowing that 8 × 5 = 40, one knows 40 ÷ 5 = 8) or properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can multiply and divide numbers up to 100 easily, using helpful strategies like knowing how multiplication and division are connected.

subject: Math
description: Multiply and divide within 100.
description_plain: Your child understands how to multiply and divide numbers up to 100.

subject: Math
description: Understand division as an unknown-factor problem. For example, find 32÷8 by finding the number that makes 32 when multiplied by 8.
description_plain: Your child can solve division problems by thinking about what number times the divisor equals the dividend, like finding 32 ÷ 8 by asking what times 8 is 32.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to multiply and divide. (Students need not use formal terms for these properties.)
description_plain: Your child uses simple rules about numbers to help multiply and divide more easily, even if they don’t know the formal names for these rules.

subject: Math
description: Determine the unknown whole number in a multiplication or division equation relating three whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can find the missing number in multiplication or division equations with three whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Use multiplication and division within 100 to solve word problems in situations involving equal groups, arrays, and measurement quantities, e.g., by using drawings and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems using multiplication and division with numbers up to 100, especially when dealing with equal groups, arrays, or measurements.

subject: Math
description: Interpret whole-number quotients of whole numbers, e.g., interpret 56 ÷ 8 as the number of objects in each share when 56 objects are partitioned equally into 8 shares, or as a number of shares when 56 objects are partitioned into equal shares of 8 objects each.
description_plain: Your child understands what division means, like sharing 56 objects equally into 8 groups or finding how many groups of 8 fit into 56.

subject: Math
description: Interpret products of whole numbers, e.g., interpret 5x7 as the total number of objects in 5 groups of 7 objects each. For example, describe a context in which a total number of objects can be expressed as 5x7.
description_plain: Your child understands multiplication as putting equal groups together, like knowing 5 x 7 means 5 groups of 7 objects each.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Compare two decimals to hundredths by reasoning about their size. Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two decimals refer to the same whole. Record the results of comparisons with the symbols >, <, or =, and justify the conclusions.
description_plain: Your child can compare two decimals to hundredths by thinking about their size, write >, =, or < to show the comparison, and explain why, using pictures or models.

subject: Math
description: Read and write decimal notation for fractions with denominators 10 or 100. Locate these decimals on a number line.
description_plain: Your child can read and write decimals for fractions with denominators of 10 or 100 and can find these decimals on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Express a fraction with denominator 10 as an equivalent fraction with denominator 100, and use this technique to add two fractions with respective denominators 10 and 100. For example, express 3/10 as 30/100, and add 3/10 + 4/100 = 34/100.
description_plain: Your child can change a fraction with denominator 10 into an equal fraction with denominator 100, and use this to add fractions with denominators 10 and 100.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving multiplication of a fraction by a whole number, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem. For example, if each person at a party will eat 3/8 of a pound of roast beef, and there will be 5 people at the party, how many pounds of roast beef will be needed? Between what two whole numbers does your answer lie?
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that multiply a fraction by a whole number, using pictures or equations, and figure out between which two whole numbers the answer falls.

subject: Math
description: Understand a multiple of a/b as a multiple of 1/b, and use this understanding to multiply a fraction by a whole number. For example, use a visual fraction model to express 3 x (2/5) as 6 x (1/5), recognizing this product as 6/5. (In general, n x (a/b) = (n x a)/b = (n x a) x 1/b.)
description_plain: Your child understands that multiplying a fraction by a whole number means multiplying the numerator and can use this to find the product.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction a/b as a multiple of 1/b. For example, use a visual fraction model to represent 5/4 as the product 5 x (¼), recording the conclusion by the equation 5/4 = 5 x (¼).
description_plain: Your child understands a fraction like 5/4 as 5 times 1/4, and can show this using pictures and equations.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication to multiply a fraction by a whole number.
description_plain: Your child can multiply a fraction by a whole number using what they already know about multiplication.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions referring to the same whole and having like denominators, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that add or subtract fractions with the same denominator.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract mixed numbers with like denominators, e.g., by replacing each mixed number with an equivalent fraction, and/or by using properties of operations and the relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract mixed numbers with the same bottom number by changing them into improper fractions or using addition and subtraction rules.

subject: Math
description: Decompose a fraction into a sum of fractions with like denominators in more than one way, recording each decomposition by an equation. Justify decompositions, e.g., by using a visual fraction model.
description_plain: Your child can break a fraction into smaller fractions with the same bottom number in different ways and explain their thinking using pictures.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract of fractions e.g., joining and separating parts referring to the same whole.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract fractions by putting parts together or taking parts away from the same whole.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction a/b with a > 1 as a sum of fractions 1/b. For example, 4/5 = 1/5 + 1/5 + 1/5 + 1/5
description_plain: Your child understands that a fraction like 4/5 is the same as adding 1/5 four times.

subject: Math
description: Compare two fractions with different numerators and different denominators, by creating common denominators or numerators, or by comparing to a benchmark fraction such as ½. Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two fractions refer to the same whole. Record the results of comparisons with symbols <, >, =, and justify the conclusions.
description_plain: Your child can compare two fractions with different top and bottom numbers by finding common numbers or comparing to ½, and can explain which is bigger or smaller.

subject: Math
description: Explain why a fraction a/b is equivalent to a fraction (n × a)/(n × b) by using visual fraction models, with attention to how the number and size of the parts differ even though the two fractions themselves are the same size. Use this principle to recognize and generate equivalent fractions.
description_plain: Your child can explain why two fractions are equal by using pictures that show how the parts are different sizes but the fractions are the same.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding to round multi-digit whole numbers to any place.
description_plain: Your child can round large whole numbers to any place value using their understanding of place value.

subject: Math
description: Compare two multi-digit numbers based on values of the digits in each place, using <, >, and = symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can compare two numbers with many digits by looking at the value of each digit and use <, >, or = to show which is bigger or if they are equal.

subject: Math
description: Read and write multi-digit whole numbers using base-ten numerals (standard form), number names (word form), and expanded form.
description_plain: Your child can read and write large whole numbers in regular number form, word form, and expanded form.

subject: Math
description: Read and write multi-digit whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can read and write large whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that in a multi-digit whole number, a digit in one place represents ten times what it represents in the place to its right. For example, recognize that the 7 in 700 is 10 times greater than the 7 in 70 because 700 ÷ 70 =10 and 70 x 10=700.
description_plain: Your child understands that in a number, a digit in one place is ten times bigger than the same digit in the place to its right.

subject: Math
description: Find whole-number quotients and remainders with up to four-digit dividends and one-digit divisors, using strategies based on place value, the properties of operations, and/or the relationship between multiplication and division. Illustrate and explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, and/or area models.
description_plain: Your child can divide numbers up to four digits by one-digit numbers, find the quotient and remainder, and explain the steps using pictures or equations.

subject: Math
description: Multiply a whole number of up to four digits by a one-digit whole number, and multiply two two-digit numbers, using strategies based on place value and the properties of operations. Illustrate and explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, and/or area models.
description_plain: Your child can multiply a number up to four digits by a one-digit number and multiply two two-digit numbers, explaining their work with pictures or equations.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract multi-digit whole numbers using an algorithm including, but not limited to, the standard algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract large whole numbers quickly and correctly using step-by-step methods.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and draw lines of symmetry for two-dimensional figures.
description_plain: Your child can find and draw lines of symmetry on flat shapes.

subject: Math
description: Classify two-dimensional figures based on the presence or absence of parallel or perpendicular lines, or the presence or absence of angles of a specified size. Recognize, and identify categories of right, acute, and obtuse triangles.
description_plain: Your child can sort flat shapes by whether they have parallel or perpendicular lines or certain angle sizes, and can recognize and name right triangles.

subject: Math
description: Draw points, lines, line segments, rays, angles (right, acute, obtuse), and perpendicular and parallel lines. Identify these in two-dimensional figures.
description_plain: Your child can draw points, lines, line segments, rays, different types of angles, and parallel or perpendicular lines, and find these in flat shapes.

subject: Math
description: Make a line plot to display a data set of measurements in fractions of a unit (½, ¼, 1/8). Solve problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions by using information presented in line plots.
description_plain: Your child can make a line plot to show measurements using fractions like ½, ¼, or 1/8 and solve addition and subtraction problems using the plot.

subject: Math
description: Recognize angle measure as additive. When an angle is decomposed into non-overlapping parts, the angle measure of the whole is the sum of the angle measures of the parts. Solve addition and subtraction problems to find unknown angles on a diagram in real world and mathematical problems, e.g., by using an equation with a symbol for the unknown angle measure.
description_plain: Your child understands that angles can be added together, and can solve problems to find missing angles by adding or subtracting angle measures.

subject: Math
description: Measure angles in whole-number degrees using a protractor. Sketch angles of specified measure.
description_plain: Your child can measure angles using a protractor and draw angles of a given size.

subject: Math
description: An angle that turns through in one-degree angles is said to have an angle measure of n degrees.
description_plain: Your child understands that an angle’s size is measured by how many one-degree turns it makes.

subject: Math
description: An angle is measured with reference to a circle with its center at the common endpoint of the rays, by considering the fraction of the circular arc between the points where the two rays intersect the circle. An angle that turns through 1/360 of a circle is called a "one-degree angle," and can be used to measure angles.
description_plain: Your child knows that angles are measured by how much of a circle they cover, with one degree being 1/360 of a full turn.

subject: Math
description: Recognize angles as geometric shapes that are formed wherever two rays share a common endpoint, and understand concepts of angle measurement.
description_plain: Your child understands that angles are shapes made by two rays sharing a point, and learns how to measure them.

subject: Math
description: Apply the area and perimeter formulas for rectangles in real world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas to find the area and perimeter of rectangles in real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Use the four operations to solve word problems involving distances, intervals of time, liquid volumes, masses of objects, and money, including problems involving fractions or decimals, and problems that require expressing measurements given in a larger unit in terms of a smaller unit. Represent measurement quantities using diagrams such as number line diagrams that feature a measurement scale.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with measurements like distance, time, volume, weight, and money.

subject: Math
description: Know relative sizes of measurement units within one system of units including km, m, cm; kg, g; lb, oz.; l, ml; hr, min, sec. Within a single system of measurement, express measurements in a larger unit in terms of a smaller unit. Record measurement equivalents in a two column table. For example, know that 1ft is 12 times as long as 1 in. Express the length of a 4 ft snake as 48 in. Generate a conversion table for feet and inches listing the number pairs (1, 12), (2, 24), (3, 36), …
description_plain: Your child knows how different units of measurement relate within the same system and can convert measurements from larger to smaller units and make a conversion table.

subject: Math
description: Generate a number or shape pattern that follows a given rule. Identify apparent features of the pattern that were not explicit in the rule itself. For example, given the rule "Add 3" and the starting number is 1, generate terms in the resulting sequence and observe that the terms appear to alternate between odd and even numbers. Explain informally why the numbers will continue to alternate in this way.
description_plain: Your child can create number or shape patterns that follow a rule and notice new features in the pattern.

subject: Math
description: Determine whether a given whole number is prime or composite.
description_plain: Your child can tell if a whole number is prime (only divisible by 1 and itself) or composite (has other divisors).

subject: Math
description: Determine whether a given whole number is a multiple of each of a given one-digit number.
description_plain: Your child can tell if a whole number can be divided evenly by a one-digit number.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that a whole number is a multiple of each of its factors.
description_plain: Your child understands that a whole number can be divided evenly by each of its factors.

subject: Math
description: Find all factor pairs for a given whole number.
description_plain: Your child can find all pairs of numbers that multiply together to make a given whole number.

subject: Math
description: Using whole number in the range 1–100.
description_plain: Your child works with whole numbers from 1 to 100 to understand multiplication and division.

subject: Math
description: Solve multistep word problems posed with whole numbers and having whole-number answers using the four operations, including problems in which remainders must be interpreted. Represent these problems using equations with a letter standing for the unknown quantity. Assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies including rounding.
description_plain: Your child can solve multi-step word problems with whole numbers using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division, and check if answers make sense.

subject: Math
description: Multiply or divide to solve word problems involving multiplicative comparison, e.g., by using drawings and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem), and distinguish multiplicative comparison from additive comparison.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that compare amounts by multiplying or dividing, and understand the difference between multiplicative and additive comparisons.

subject: Math
description: Know from memory (quick effortless recall of facts) all products of two one-digit numbers.
description_plain: Your child remembers all multiplication facts for one-digit numbers quickly and easily.

subject: Math
description: Interpret a multiplication equation as a comparison, e.g., interpret 35 = 5 × 7 as a statement that 35 is 5 times as many as 7 and 7 times as many as 5. Represent verbal or written statements of multiplicative comparisons as multiplication equations. Example: Tom has 7 toy cars; Joe has 5 times as many. How many toy cars does Joe have? Answer: 35, because 7 x 5 = 35 or 5 x 7 = 35.
description_plain: Your child understands multiplication as comparing amounts and can write multiplication equations from word problems.

subject: Math
description: Use and interpret multiplicative equations.
description_plain: Your child uses and understands multiplication equations.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Solve real world problems involving division of unit fractions by non-zero whole numbers and division of whole numbers by unit fractions, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem. For example, how much chocolate will each person get if 3 people share ½ lb of chocolate equally? How many 1/3-cup servings are in 2 cups of raisins?
description_plain: Your child can solve real problems dividing unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions using pictures and equations.

subject: Math
description: Interpret division of a whole number by a unit fraction, and compute such quotients. For example, create a story context for 4 ÷ (1/5), and use a visual fraction model to show the quotient. Use the relationship between multiplication and division to explain that 4 ÷ (1/5) = 20 because 20 × (1/5) = 4.
description_plain: Your child can divide a whole number by a fraction and explain the answer using stories and pictures.

subject: Math
description: Interpret division of a unit fraction by a non-zero whole number, and compute such quotients. For example, create a story context for (1/3) ÷ 4, and use a visual fraction model to show the quotient. Use the relationship between multiplication and division to explain that (1/3) ÷ 4 = 1/12 because (1/12) × 4 = 1/3.
description_plain: Your child can divide a fraction by a whole number and explain the answer using stories and pictures.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of division to divide unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions.
description_plain: Your child can divide unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions using strategies based on multiplication and division.

subject: Math
description: Solve real world problems involving multiplication of fractions and mixed numbers, e.g., by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life problems by multiplying fractions and mixed numbers using pictures or equations.

subject: Math
description: Explaining why multiplying a given number by a fraction greater than 1 results in a product greater than the given number (recognizing multiplication by whole numbers greater than 1 as a familiar case); explaining why multiplying a given number by a fraction less than 1 results in a product smaller than the given number; and relating the principle of fraction equivalence a/b = (n×a)/(n×b) to the effect of multiplying a/b by 1.
description_plain: Your child understands why multiplying by a fraction bigger than 1 makes a number bigger, and multiplying by a fraction smaller than 1 makes it smaller.

subject: Math
description: Comparing the size of a product to the size of one factor on the basis of the size of the other factor, without performing the indicated multiplication.
description_plain: Your child can compare the size of a multiplication answer to one of the numbers being multiplied, without actually multiplying.

subject: Math
description: Interpret multiplication as scaling (resizing), by:
description_plain: Your child understands multiplication as resizing or scaling numbers.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of a rectangle with fractional side lengths by tiling it with unit squares of the appropriate unit fraction side lengths, and show that the area is the same as would be found by multiplying the side lengths. Multiply fractional side lengths to find areas of rectangles, and represent fraction products as rectangular areas.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of rectangles with fractional sides by counting small squares and by multiplying the side lengths.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the product (a/b) × q as a parts of a partition of q into b equal parts; equivalently, as the result of a sequence of operations a × q ÷ b. For example, use a visual fraction model to show (2/3) × 4 = 8/3, and create a story context for this equation. Do the same with (2/3) × (4/5) = 8/15. (In general, (a/b) × (c/d) = ac/bd.)
description_plain: Your child understands multiplying a fraction by a number as taking parts of that number, using pictures to show this.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication to multiply a fraction or whole number by a fraction.
description_plain: Your child can multiply fractions and whole numbers by fractions, building on what they already know about multiplication.

subject: Math
description: Interpret a fraction as division of the numerator by the denominator (a/b = a ÷ b). Solve word problems involving division of whole numbers leading to answers in the form of fractions or mixed numbers, e.g., by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem. For example, interpret ¾ as the result of dividing 3 by 4, noting that ¾ multiplied by 4 equals 3, and that when 3 wholes are shared equally among 4 people each person has a share of size ¾. If 9 people want to share a 50-pound sack of rice equally by weight, how many pounds of rice should each person get? Between what two whole numbers does your answer lie?
description_plain: Your child understands that a fraction means dividing the top number by the bottom number and can solve word problems using this idea.

subject: Math
description: Use benchmark fractions and number sense of fractions to estimate mentally and assess the reasonableness of answers. For example, recognize an incorrect result 2/5 + ½ = 3/7, by observing that 3/7 < ½.
description_plain: Your child uses common fractions to estimate answers and check if fraction calculations make sense.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions referring to the same whole, including cases of unlike denominators, e.g., by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child solves word problems that add or subtract fractions with different denominators using pictures or equations.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions.
description_plain: Your child solves word problems that involve adding and subtracting fractions.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract fractions with unlike denominators (including mixed numbers) by replacing given fractions with equivalent fractions in such a way as to produce an equivalent sum or difference with a like denominator. It is not necessary at this grade level to simplify the sum or difference. For example, 2/3 + 5/4 = 8/12 + 15/12 = 23/12. (In general, a/b + c/d = (ad + bc)/bd.)
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract fractions with different denominators by changing them to equivalent fractions with the same denominator.

subject: Math
description: Multiply and divide decimals.
description_plain: Multiply and divide decimals.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract decimals
description_plain: Add and subtract decimals

subject: Math
description: Use the four operations with decimals to hundredths, using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; justify the reasoning used with a written explanation.
description_plain: Your child adds, subtracts, multiplies, and divides decimals to hundredths using models or drawings and explains their thinking.

subject: Math
description: Find whole-number quotients of whole numbers with up to four-digit dividends and two-digit divisors, using strategies based on place value, the properties of operations, and/or the relationship between multiplication and division. Explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, illustrations, area models, or other representations based on place value.
description_plain: Your child can divide whole numbers up to four digits by two-digit numbers using place value and multiplication ideas, and explain their work with pictures or equations.

subject: Math
description: Fluently multiply multi-digit whole numbers using an algorithm, including but not limited to the standard algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can multiply large whole numbers using step-by-step methods.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding to round decimals to any place.
description_plain: Your child can round decimal numbers to any place value using their understanding of place value.

subject: Math
description: Compare two decimals to thousandths based on meanings of the digits in each place, using >, =, and, < symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child compares two decimals up to three decimal places and uses >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.

subject: Math
description: Read and write decimals to thousandths using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form, e.g., 347.392 = 3 × 100 + 4 × 10 + 7 × 1 + 3 × (1/10) + 9 × (1/100) + 2 × (1/1000).
description_plain: Your child can read and write decimals to the thousandths place in numbers, words, and expanded form.

subject: Math
description: Read, write, and compare decimals to thousandths.
description_plain: Your child can read, write, and compare decimals up to the thousandths place.

subject: Math
description: Explain and apply patterns in the number of zeros of the product when multiplying a number by powers of 10. Explain and apply patterns in the placement of the decimal point with respect to the values of the digits in the product or the quotient, when a decimal is multiplied or divided by a power of 10. Use whole-number exponents to denote powers of 10.
description_plain: Your child understands how multiplying or dividing by 10, 100, or 1000 changes numbers and where the decimal point moves.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that in a multi-digit number, a digit in one place represents 10 times as much as it represents in the place to its right and 1/10 of what it represents in the place to its left.
description_plain: Your child knows that in a number, each digit is ten times bigger than the digit to its right and one-tenth the size of the digit to its left.

subject: Math
description: Classify two-dimensional figures in a hierarchy based on properties.
description_plain: Your child can sort flat shapes into groups based on their features, showing how some shapes are part of bigger groups.

subject: Math
description: Understand that attributes belonging to a category of two-dimensional figures also belong to all subcategories of that category. For example, all rectangles have four right angles and squares are rectangles, so all squares have four right angles.
description_plain: Your child understands that if a shape belongs to a group, it also has all the features of that group.

subject: Math
description: Represent real world and mathematical problems by graphing points in the first quadrant of the coordinate plane, and interpret coordinate values of points in the context of the situation.
description_plain: Your child can plot points on the first quadrant of a coordinate plane and explain what the points mean in real situations.

subject: Math
description: Use a pair of perpendicular number lines, called axes, to define a coordinate system, with the intersection of the lines (the origin) arranged to coincide with the 0 on each line and a given point in the plane located by using an ordered pair of numbers, called its coordinates. Understand that the first number indicates how far to travel from the origin in the direction of one axis, and the second number indicates how far to travel in the direction of the second axis, with the convention that the names of the two axes and the coordinates correspond (e.g., x-axis and x-coordinate, y-axis and y-coordinate).
description_plain: Your child understands how to use two number lines that cross to find and name points using pairs of numbers called coordinates.

subject: Math
description: Use information from a line plot representing an unequal situation and redistribute whole or fractional parts to create an equal distribution. For example, given different measurements of liquid in identical beakers, find the amount of liquid each beaker would contain if the total amount in all the beakers were redistributed equally
description_plain: Your child uses data from a line plot to share amounts equally, like dividing liquid evenly into containers.

subject: Math
description: Use operations on fractions of a unit (½, ¼, 1/8) for this grade to solve problems involving information presented in line plots.
description_plain: Your child uses fractions like halves, quarters, and eighths to solve problems from line plots.

subject: Math
description: Make a line plot to display a data set.
description_plain: Your child makes a line plot to show a set of data.

subject: Math
description: Recognize volume as additive. Find volumes of solid figures composed of two non-overlapping right rectangular prisms by adding the volumes of the non-overlapping parts, applying this technique to solve real world problems.
description_plain: Your child finds the total volume of shapes made by putting two rectangular boxes together by adding their volumes.

subject: Math
description: Apply the formulas V = l × w × h and V = B × h (where B is the area of the base) for rectangular prisms to find volumes of right rectangular prisms with whole number edge lengths in the context of solving real world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas to find the volume of rectangular boxes with whole number sides when solving real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Represent threefold whole-number products as volumes, e.g., to represent the associative property of multiplication.
description_plain: Your child can show how multiplying three whole numbers relates to finding volume, helping them understand how multiplication works together.

subject: Math
description: Find the volume of a right rectangular prism with whole-number side lengths by packing it with unit cubes, and show that the volume is the same as would be found by multiplying the edge lengths, equivalently by multiplying the height by the area of the base.
description_plain: Your child can find the volume of a rectangular box by filling it with unit cubes and show that multiplying its length, width, and height gives the same volume.

subject: Math
description: Relate volume to the operations of multiplication and addition and solve real world and mathematical problems involving volume.
description_plain: Your child understands that volume can be found by multiplying and adding, and can solve problems about volume.

subject: Math
description: Measure volumes by counting unit cubes, using cubic cm, cubic in, cubic ft, and improvised units.
description_plain: Your child can measure volume by counting unit cubes and use different units like cubic centimeters or cubic inches.

subject: Math
description: A solid figure which can be packed without gaps or overlaps using n unit cubes is said to have a volume of n cubic units.
description_plain: Your child knows that if a solid can be filled exactly with n unit cubes, its volume is n cubic units.

subject: Math
description: A cube with side length 1 unit, called a "unit cube," is said to have "one cubic unit" of volume, and can be used to measure volume.
description_plain: Your child understands that a cube with sides of 1 unit is called a unit cube and has a volume of one cubic unit.

subject: Math
description: Recognize volume as an attribute of solid figures and understand concepts of volume measurement.
description_plain: Your child understands that volume is how much space a solid figure takes up and learns how to measure it.

subject: Math
description: Convert customary and metric measurement units within a given measurement system (e.g., convert 5 cm to 0.05 m). Use these conversions in solving multi-step, real world problems involving distances, intervals of time, liquid volumes, masses of objects, and money (including problems involving simple fractions or decimals). For example, 3.6 liters and 4.1 liters can be combined as 7.7 liters or 7700 milliliters.
description_plain: Your child can change measurements from one unit to another within the same system, like centimeters to meters, and use these changes to solve real-world problems involving length, time, volume, weight, and money.

subject: Math
description: Generate two numerical patterns using two given rules. Form ordered pairs consisting of corresponding terms from the two patterns, and graph the ordered pairs on a coordinate plane. Identify the relationship between the two patterns. For example, given the rule "Add 3" and the starting number 0, and given the rule "Add 6" and the starting number 0, generate terms in the resulting sequences, and observe that the terms in one sequence are twice the corresponding terms in the other sequence. Explain informally why this is so.
description_plain: Your child can create two number patterns from given rules, pair their matching terms, plot these pairs on a graph, and explain how the two patterns are connected.

subject: Math
description: Write simple expressions that record calculations with numbers to represent real world problems, and interpret numerical expressions without evaluating them.(For example, express the calculation "add 8 and 7, then multiply by 2" as 2 × (8 + 7). Recognize that 3 × (18932 + 921) is three times as large as 18932 + 921, without having to calculate the indicated sum or product.)
description_plain: Your child can write math expressions to show real-life calculations and understand what these expressions mean without always solving them.

subject: Math
description: Use and explain parentheses, in numerical expressions, and evaluate expressions with these symbols.
description_plain: Your child can use parentheses in math expressions to show which operations to do first and can solve expressions that include them.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Relating the choice of measures of center and variability to the shape of the data distribution and the context in which the data were gathered.
description_plain: Your child can choose the best way to describe data based on its shape and the situation it came from.

subject: Math
description: Giving quantitative measures of center (median and/or mean) and variability (interquartile range and/or mean absolute deviation), as well as describing any overall pattern and any striking deviations from the overall pattern with reference to the context in which the data were gathered.
description_plain: Your child can describe the middle and spread of data numbers and point out any unusual values, explaining what the data is about.

subject: Math
description: Describing the nature of the attribute under investigation, including how it was measured and its units of measurement.
description_plain: Your child can describe what is being measured in data, how it was measured, and the units used.

subject: Math
description: Reporting the number of observations.
description_plain: Your child can tell how many data points or observations there are in a set.

subject: Math
description: Summarize numerical data sets in relation to their context, such as by:
description_plain: Your child can summarize sets of numbers by explaining what they mean in their real-life context.

subject: Math
description: Display numerical data in plots on a number line, including dot plots, histograms, and box plots.
description_plain: Your child can show number data on graphs like dot plots, histograms, and box plots using a number line.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that a measure of center (mean and/or median) for a numerical data set summarizes all of its values with a single number, while a measure of variation (such as mean absolute deviation and/or range) summarizes data points' distances from the mean or each other.
description_plain: Your child understands that the average (mean or median) summarizes a set of numbers with one value, while measures like range or mean absolute deviation show how spread out the numbers are.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a set of data collected to answer a statistical question has a distribution which can be described by its center spread, and overall shape.
description_plain: Your child understands that data collected to answer a question can be described by its center, spread, and overall shape.

subject: Math
description: Recognize a statistical question as one that anticipates variability in the data related to the question and accounts for it in the answers.
description_plain: Your child knows that a good statistical question expects different answers and includes that in how it is answered.

subject: Math
description: Use variables to represent two quantities in a real-world problem that change in relationship to one another; write an equation to express one quantity, thought of as the dependent variable, in terms of the other quantity, thought of as the independent variable. Analyze the relationship between the dependent and independent variables using graphs and tables, and relate these to the equation.
description_plain: Your child can use letters to show how two changing amounts relate, write equations for them, and use graphs and tables to understand the relationship.

subject: Math
description: Write an inequality of the form x > c, x ≥ c, x < c or x ≤ c which represents a condition or constraint in a real-world or mathematical problem. Recognize that inequalities have infinitely many solutions; represent solutions of inequalities on number line diagrams.
description_plain: Your child can write inequalities to show limits or conditions in problems and can show all possible solutions on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems by writing and solving equations of the form x + p = q and px = q for cases in which p, q and x are all nonnegative rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems by writing and solving simple equations like x + p = q or p × x = q.

subject: Math
description: Use variables to represent numbers and write expressions when solving a real-world or mathematical problem; understand that a variable can represent an unknown number, or, depending on the purpose at hand, any number in a specified set.
description_plain: Your child can use letters to stand for numbers and write expressions to solve problems, knowing the letter can mean an unknown or any number in a set.

subject: Math
description: Understand solving an equation or inequality is a process in which you determine values from a set that make an equation or inequality true. Use substitution to determine whether a given number in a specified set makes an equation or inequality true.
description_plain: Your child knows that solving equations or inequalities means finding numbers that make them true and can check if a number works by plugging it in.

subject: Math
description: Identify when two expressions are equivalent (i.e., when the two expressions name the same number regardless of which value is substituted into them).
description_plain: Your child can tell when two math expressions are equal because they give the same answer no matter what number is used.

subject: Math
description: Apply the properties of operations to generate equivalent expressions with an emphasis on the distributive property.
description_plain: Your child can use math rules, especially the distributive property, to rewrite expressions in different but equal ways.

subject: Math
description: Perform arithmetic operations following the order of operations with and without parentheses, including those involving whole-number exponents.
description_plain: Your child can do math operations in the right order, including working with exponents and parentheses.

subject: Math
description: Evaluate expressions at specific values of their variables. Include expressions that arise from formulas used in real-world problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the value of math expressions by plugging in numbers, including those from real-life formulas, and follow the correct order of operations.

subject: Math
description: Identify parts of an expression using mathematical terms (sum, term, product, factor, quotient, coefficient); view one or more parts of an expression as a single entity. For example, describe the expression 2 (8 + 7) as a product of two factors; view (8 +7) as both a single entity and a sum of two terms.
description_plain: Your child can name parts of math expressions like sum, product, and factor, and see parts of an expression as one unit.

subject: Math
description: Write expressions that record operations with numbers and with letters standing for numbers. For example, express the calculation "Subtract y from 5" as 5-y.
description_plain: Your child can write math expressions using numbers and letters to stand for numbers, like writing '5 minus y' as 5 - y.

subject: Math
description: Write, read, and evaluate expressions in which letters stand for numbers.
description_plain: Your child can read, write, and solve math expressions that use letters to represent numbers.

subject: Math
description: Write and evaluate numerical expressions involving whole-number exponents (e.g. parentheses, brackets, or braces).
description_plain: Your child can write and solve math expressions that include whole-number exponents and grouping symbols like parentheses.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems by graphing points in all four quadrants of the coordinate plane. Include use of coordinates and absolute value to find distances between points with the same first coordinate or the same second coordinate.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems by graphing points in all four parts of the coordinate plane and find distances between points using coordinates and absolute value.

subject: Math
description: Distinguish comparisons of absolute value from statements about order.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between comparing absolute values and comparing the order of numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand the absolute value of a rational number as its distance from 0 on the number line; interpret absolute value as magnitude for a positive or negative quantity in a real-world situation.
description_plain: Your child understands absolute value as how far a number is from zero and can explain it in real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: Write, interpret, and explain statements of order for rational numbers in real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child can write and explain how numbers compare in order using real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Interpret statements of inequality as statements about the relative position of two numbers on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can understand inequality statements as showing where two numbers are located on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Understand ordering and absolute value of rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child understands how to order rational numbers and what absolute value means.

subject: Math
description: Find and position integers and other rational numbers on a horizontal or vertical number line diagram; find and position pairs of integers and other rational numbers on a coordinate plane.
description_plain: Your child can find and place integers and rational numbers on number lines and coordinate planes.

subject: Math
description: Understand signs of numbers in ordered pairs as indicating locations in quadrants of the coordinate plane; recognize that when two ordered pairs differ only by signs, the locations of the points are related by reflections across one or both axes.
description_plain: Your child understands how the signs of numbers in pairs show locations in different parts of the coordinate plane and how changing signs reflects points across axes.

subject: Math
description: Recognize opposite signs of numbers as indicating locations on opposite sides of 0 on the number line; recognize that the opposite of the opposite of a number is the number itself, e.g., –(–3) = 3, and that 0 is its own opposite.
description_plain: Your child knows that opposite signs mean numbers are on opposite sides of zero and that the opposite of the opposite number is the original number.

subject: Math
description: Understand a rational number as a point on the number line. Extend number line diagrams and coordinate axes familiar from previous grades to represent points on the line and in the plane with negative number coordinates.
description_plain: Your child understands that rational numbers can be shown as points on number lines and coordinate planes, including negative numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand that positive and negative numbers are used together to describe quantities having opposite directions or values (e.g., temperature above/below zero, elevation above/below sea level, credits/debits, positive/negative electric charge); use positive and negative numbers to represent quantities in real-world contexts, explaining the meaning of 0 in each situation.
description_plain: Your child understands positive and negative numbers as showing opposite directions or values, like temperatures above or below zero, and can explain what zero means in these situations.

subject: Math
description: Find the greatest common factor of two whole numbers less than or equal to 100 and the least common multiple of two whole numbers less than or equal to 12. Use the distributive property to express a sum of two whole numbers 1–100 with a common factor as a multiple of a sum of two whole numbers with no common factor.
description_plain: Your child can find the greatest common factor and least common multiple of numbers and use the distributive property to rewrite sums with common factors.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add, subtract, multiply, and divide multi-digit decimals using an algorithm including but not limited to the standard algorithm for each operation.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals with many digits using step-by-step methods.

subject: Math
description: Fluently divide multi-digit numbers using an algorithm including but not limited to the standard algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can divide large numbers using step-by-step methods accurately and efficiently.

subject: Math
description: Interpret and compute quotients of fractions, and solve word problems involving division of fractions by fractions, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem. For example, create a story context for (2/3) ÷ (¾) and use a visual fraction model to show the quotient; use the relationship between multiplication and division to explain that (2/3) ÷ (¾) = 8/9 because ¾ of 8/9 is 2/3. (In general, (a/b) ÷ (c/d) = ad/bc.) How much chocolate will each person get if 3 people share ½ lb of chocolate equally? How many ¾-cup servings are in 2/3 of a cup of yogurt? How wide is a rectangular strip of land with length ¾ mi and area ½ square mi?
description_plain: Your child can divide fractions by fractions and solve word problems about it using pictures and equations.

subject: Math
description: Use ratio reasoning to convert measurement units; manipulate and transform units appropriately when multiplying or dividing quantities.
description_plain: Your child can use ratio thinking to change measurement units correctly when multiplying or dividing.

subject: Math
description: Find a percent of a quantity as a rate per 100 (e.g., 30% of a quantity means 30/100 times the quantity); solve problems involving finding the whole, given a part and the percent.
description_plain: Your child can find a percent of a number and solve problems where you know the part and the percent to find the whole.

subject: Math
description: Solve unit rate problems including those involving unit pricing and constant speed. For example, if it took 7 hours to mow 4 lawns, then at that rate, how many lawns could be mowed in 35 hours? At what rate were lawns being mowed?
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about rates, like figuring out how many lawns can be mowed in a certain time or the speed of mowing.

subject: Math
description: Make tables of equivalent ratios relating quantities with whole number measurements, find missing values in the tables, and plot the pairs of values on the coordinate plane. Use tables to compare ratios.
description_plain: Your child can make tables of equivalent ratios, find missing numbers, and graph these pairs to compare ratios.

subject: Math
description: Use ratio and rate reasoning to solve real-world and mathematical problems, e.g., by reasoning about tables of equivalent ratios, tape diagrams, double number line diagrams, or equations.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems using ratios and rates, by looking at tables, diagrams, or equations.

subject: Math
description: Understand the concept of a unit rate a/b associated with a ratio a:b with b not equal to 0, and use rate language in the context of a ratio relationship. For example, "This recipe has a ratio of 3 cups of flour to 4 cups of sugar, so there is ¾ cup of flour for each cup of sugar." "We paid $75 for 15 hamburgers, which is a rate of $5 per hamburger."
description_plain: Your child understands what a unit rate is and can explain it using examples, like how much flour is used per cup of sugar or the price per hamburger.

subject: Math
description: Understand the concept of a ratio and use ratio language to describe a ratio relationship between two quantities. For examples, "The ratio of wings to beaks in the bird house at the zoo was 2:1, because for every 2 wings there was 1 beak." "For every vote candidate A received, candidate C received nearly three votes."
description_plain: Your child understands what a ratio is and can describe the relationship between two amounts using ratio language.

subject: Math
description: Represent three-dimensional figures using nets made up of rectangles and triangles, and use the nets to find the surface area of these figures. Apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can draw flat patterns (nets) made of rectangles and triangles to show 3D shapes and use these to find surface area in real-life problems.

subject: Math
description: Draw polygons in the coordinate plane given coordinates for the vertices; use coordinates to find the length of a side joining points with the same first coordinate or the same second coordinate. Apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can draw shapes on a coordinate grid using points and find side lengths when points share the same x or y value, applying this to real problems.

subject: Math
description: Find the volume of a right rectangular prism with fractional edge lengths by packing it with unit cubes of the appropriate unit fraction edge lengths, and show that the volume is the same as would be found by multiplying the edge lengths of the prism. Apply the formulas V = lwh and V = Bh where B is the area of the base to find volumes of right rectangular prisms with fractional edge lengths in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the volume of rectangular boxes with fractional side lengths by filling them with smaller cubes and by multiplying the edges, using formulas in real-life problems.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of right triangles, other triangles, special quadrilaterals, and polygons by composing into rectangles or decomposing into triangles and other shapes; apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of triangles, special four-sided shapes, and other polygons by breaking them into simpler shapes.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Design and use a simulation to generate frequencies for compound events. For example, use random digits as a simulation tool to approximate the answer to the question: If 40% of donors have type A blood, what is the probability that it will take at least 4 donors to find one with type A blood?
description_plain: Your child can create and use simulations, like using random numbers, to estimate how often combined events happen.

subject: Math
description: Represent sample spaces for compound events using methods such as organized lists, tables and tree diagrams. For an event described in everyday language (e.g., "rolling double sixes"), identify the outcomes in the sample space which compose the event.
description_plain: Your child can list all possible outcomes for combined events using organized methods and identify which outcomes match a described event.

subject: Math
description: Understand that, just as with simple events, the probability of a compound event is the fraction of outcomes in the sample space for which the compound event occurs.
description_plain: Your child understands that the chance of combined events happening is the fraction of all possible outcomes that fit the event.

subject: Math
description: Find probabilities of compound events using organized lists, tables, tree diagrams, and simulation.
description_plain: Your child can find the probability of combined events using lists, tables, tree diagrams, and simulations.

subject: Math
description: Develop a probability model (which may not be uniform) by observing frequencies in data generated from a chance process. For example, find the approximate probability that a spinning penny will land heads up or that a tossed paper cup will land open-end down. Do the outcomes from the spinning penny appear to be equally likely based on the observed frequencies?
description_plain: Your child can create a probability model by watching how often things happen in chance activities, even if outcomes are not all equally likely.

subject: Math
description: Develop a uniform probability model by assigning equal probability to all outcomes, and use the model to determine probabilities of events. For example, if a student is selected at random from a class, find the probability that Jane will be selected and the probability that a girl will be selected.
description_plain: Your child can make a probability model where all outcomes are equally likely and use it to find the chance of events.

subject: Math
description: Develop a probability model and use it to find probabilities of events. Compare probabilities from a model to observed frequencies; if the agreement is not good, explain possible sources of the discrepancy.
description_plain: Your child can build a probability model to find chances of events and compare these to actual results, explaining why they might be different.

subject: Math
description: Approximate the probability of a chance event by collecting data on the chance process that produces it and observing its long-run relative frequency, and predict the approximate relative frequency given the probability. For example, when rolling a number cube 600 times, predict that a 3 or 6 would be rolled roughly 200 times, but probably not exactly 200 times.
description_plain: Your child can estimate the chance of an event by collecting data and observing how often it happens over time, then predict future chances.

subject: Math
description: Understand that the probability of a chance event is a number between 0 and 1 that expresses the likelihood of the event occurring. Larger numbers indicate greater likelihood. A probability near 0 indicates an unlikely event, a probability around ½ indicates an event that is neither unlikely nor likely, and a probability near 1 indicates a likely event.
description_plain: Your child understands that probability is a number from 0 to 1 showing how likely an event is, with numbers near 0 meaning unlikely and near 1 meaning very likely.

subject: Math
description: Use measures of center and measures of variability for numerical data from random samples to draw informal comparative inferences about two populations. For example, decide whether the words in a chapter of a seventh-grade science book are generally longer than the words in a chapter of a fourth-grade science book.
description_plain: Your child can use averages and spread of data from random samples to make simple comparisons between two groups.

subject: Math
description: Informally assess the degree of visual overlap of two numerical data distributions with similar variabilities, using quantitative measures of center (focusing on mean and median) and variability (interquartile range, mean absolute deviation), as well as describing any overall pattern and any striking deviations from the overall pattern with reference to the context in which the data were gathered. For example, the mean height of players on the basketball team is 10 cm greater than the mean height of players on the soccer team, about twice the variability (mean absolute deviation) on either team; on a dot plot, the separation between the two distributions of heights is noticeable.
description_plain: Your child can look at two sets of data and estimate how much their values overlap and how different their centers are compared to their spread.

subject: Math
description: Use data from a random sample to draw inferences about a population with an unknown characteristic of interest. Generate multiple samples (or simulated samples) of the same size to gauge the variation in estimates or predictions. For example, estimate the mean word length in a book by randomly sampling words from the book; predict the winner of a school election based on randomly sampled survey data. Gauge how far off the estimate or prediction might be.
description_plain: Your child can use data from random samples to make guesses about a larger group and create several samples to see how much the guesses might change.

subject: Math
description: Understand that statistics can be used to gain information about a population by examining a sample of the population; generalizations about a population from a sample are valid only if the sample is representative of that population. Understand that random sampling tends to produce representative samples and support valid inferences.
description_plain: Your child understands that looking at a smaller group (sample) can tell us about a bigger group (population) only if the sample fairly represents the whole group.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems leading to inequalities of the form px + q > r, px + q ≥ r, px + q < r, and px + q ≤ r where p, q, and r are specific rational numbers. Graph the solution set of the inequality and interpret it in the context of the problem. For example, as a salesperson, you are paid $50 per week plus $3 per sale. This week you want your pay to be at least $100. Write an inequality for the number of sales you need to make, and describe the solutions.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that lead to inequalities, graph the answers, and explain what the solutions mean in real life.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems leading to equations of the form px + q = r and p(x + q) = r, where p, q, and r are specific rational numbers. Solve equations of these forms fluently. Compare an algebraic solution to an arithmetic solution, identifying the sequence of the operations used in each approach.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that lead to equations like px + q = r or p(x + q) = r, solve them easily, and compare algebra and arithmetic methods.

subject: Math
description: Use variables to represent quantities in a real-world or mathematical problem, and construct simple equations and inequalities to solve problems by reasoning about the quantities.
description_plain: Your child can use letters to stand for numbers in real or math problems and write simple equations or inequalities to solve them.

subject: Math
description: Assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies. For example, if you want to place a towel bar 9 ¾ inches long in the center of a door that is 27 ½ inches wide, you will need to place the bar about 9 inches from each edge; this estimate can be used as a check on the exact computation.
description_plain: Your child can check if answers make sense by estimating or doing quick mental math, like figuring out where to place a towel bar on a door.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations to calculate with numbers in any form; convert between forms as appropriate. For example, if a woman making $25 an hour gets a 10% raise, she will make an additional 1/10 of her salary an hour, or $2.50, for a new salary of $27.50.
description_plain: Your child can use math rules to work with numbers in different forms and change between forms when needed, like figuring out a raise on an hourly wage.

subject: Math
description: Solve multi-step real-life and mathematical problems posed with positive and negative rational numbers in any form (whole numbers, fractions, and decimals), using tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can solve multi-step problems with positive and negative numbers in different forms, using tools and checking if answers make sense.

subject: Math
